figures.caption: Figure 1: Comparison of existing layer pruning paradigms. (a) Similarity-based pruning measures local cosine similarity between adjacent layers. (b) Signal-driven pruning relies on static magnitude or activation statistics. (c) The proposed topology-aware pruning framework models layer-wise representations as point clouds and captures their global evolution via zigzag filtration, preserving transition-critical layers while pruning structurally redundant ones.

Table 1: Performance comparison of different pruning methods on the LLaVA-NeXT at a 50% sparsity ratio across multiple multimodal evaluation benchmarks. The optimal and sub-optimal scores are highlighted in bold and underlined, respectively.
Table 7: Performance comparison of different pruning methods on Qwen2-VL-7B at a 50% sparsity ratio. The best and the second best results are in bold and underlined, respectively.

Table 6: Comparison of pruning techniques on the LLaVA-OneVision model with 60% sparsity ratio and estimate performance on various multimodal evaluation benchmarks. The best and the second best results are in bold and underlined, respectively.

Table 2: Performance comparison of different pruning methods on the VideoLLaMA2 at a 60% sparsity ratio across multiple multimodal evaluation benchmarks. The optimal and sub-optimal scores are highlighted in bold and underlined, respectively.

Figure 2: Overview of the topology-adaptive pruning pipeline. Left: given an image–instruction pair, the LVLM produces layer-wise hidden states. We insert a single-token summary prompt “[RET]” to aggregate multimodal information. Right: the hidden states are converted into point clouds and fed to a zigzag filtration. The resulting effective persistence image yields inter-layer topological consistency S̄p(ℓ). Layers whose S̄p(ℓ) ≥ ϵ ∗ S̄p max are marked for pruning.

Figure 3: Topological layer characterization and sparsity–performance behavior. Layer-wise topological activity and inter-layer consistency exhibit clear stage-wise variations across model depth, and topology-aware pruning consistently outperforms existing methods across all sparsity ratios on both LLaVA-NeXT and VideoLLaMA2.
Table 3: Comparisons of inference efficiency across different pruning methods. The best results are highlighted in bold.

Table 9: Comparison of pruning techniques on the LLaVA-NeXT model with 50% sparsity ratio. Reported values are the average over three different calibration sets. The best and the second best results are in bold and underlined, respectively.

Table 10: Standard deviation of the performance of pruning techniques on the LLaVA-NeXT model with 50% sparsity ratio. Reported values are the average over three different calibration sets. The best and the second best results are in bold and underlined, respectively.
